source_metadata.title_raw: Barry Jodatian - A-19-099 - September 16, 2019 - San Francisco

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 16, 2019

Barry Jodatian
Associate Vice President
1600 Holloway Avenue
San Francisco, CA 94132

Re:     Your Request for Advice
         Our File No. A-19-099

Dear Mr. Jodatian:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090, et seq.¹ We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

     Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Francisco District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

     Does Section 1090 prohibit San Francisco State University (University) from contracting with architect Skidmore, Ownings and Merrill (SOM) to provide architectural design services related to design of a new science replacement building where SOM had previously provided programming and a conceptual design study (Feasibility Study) for an academic building, but the project has changed significantly since SOM's Feasibility Study.

CONCLUSION

     No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the University from entering a contract with SOM on the project. SOM did not engage in any planning, preliminary discussions or negotiations with the University regarding the new project site, and it did not impose considerable influence over the County regarding the new contract.

_______________________________
¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-099
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTER

A.     2017 Feasibility Study by SOM.

On October 2, 2015, the University issued an RFQ for services related to a Feasibility Study for a science replacement building to develop a building program and conceptual design and to provide a cost estimate. Addendum No. 1 to the RFQ issued on October 21, 2015, included the following question from prospective firm(s) and the University's answer:

Q. Once this study is completed, what are next steps? Will the selected firm be expected to continue into follow-on design, documentation, and construction phases, or will there be a separate RFO/P process? Alternatively, will the selected firm be precluded from the ability to pursue future phases?

A. Once the feasibility and programming study is complete, we will select the design architect for the building through a separate solicitation process. The consultant selected for the feasibility and programming study is eligible to pursue design of the building.

On June 20, 2016, the University executed a contract with SOM. The Feasibility Study, which SOM completed in May 2017, under the direction of the University, called for a 4-story, 162,000 gross-square foot building that was non-departmental and focused primarily on research. The proposed site was located in the northeast portion of the campus and the total project cost was estimated at approximately $300M.

B.     The Current Project After Project Changes in 2018.

Given the high cost of the project and a shift in programmatic emphasis, in the spring of 2018, the University engaged a different consultant, Mode Associates, to help revise the program to bring it within a lower budget and to include instructional, as well as some research space.

As currently proposed, the project is a 5-story, 105,000 gross-square-foot building, housing the departments of Engineering and Chemistry and Biochemistry, for a total project budget of $150M. The project site has changed, with the building now located on the site of the existing science building, a portion of which will remain and be connected to the new building.

With the assistance of Mode Associates, the University is refining the building program and developing design standards that will constitute a design criteria/basis of design document that will be part of the collaborative design build RFP. The original Feasibility Study prepared by SOM will not be included in the RFP, since it is no longer the defining document.

The University plans to issue a collaborative design build RFQ for a contractor-architect team in May 2019, and issue an RFP to a shortlisted group of teams in the fall of 2019, after which the project contractor and architect team will be selected.

File No. A-19-099
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

A.     Independent Contractors under Section 1090.

     Interpreting the term "employees" as used in Section 1090, the California Supreme Court affirmed the long-standing rule from case law that independent contractors are not categorically excluded from Section 1090: "Liability under the statute can extend to independent contractors who have duties to engage in or advise on public contracting." (People v. Superior Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230, 233.) For example, an independent contractor for a state or local government agency that "has a hand in designing and developing the plans and specifications for the project," has made or participated in the making of a contract for the construction of the project and is therefore prohibited from entering a contract to complete the project. (Davis v. Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300-301.)

     However, not all independent contractors are covered by Section 1090. (Sahlolbei, supra, at p. 240.) "An individual's status as an official under [Section 1090] turns on the extent to which a person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a capacity that demands the public trust." (Ibid, quoting Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (Hub City) (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1125.) In Hub City, the court held that an independent contractor that exerts "considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency" is subject to Section 1090. (Hub City, supra, at pp. 1124-1125.)

     Previously, we have advised that consultants who play a limited technical role and are removed from influencing the contracting decisions of a public agency may not be subject to Section 1090. (La Salle Advice Letter, No. A-17-074 and Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084.) Likewise, in the Chadwick Advice Letter, No. A-15-147, we determined that only the primary consultant and a "highly involved sub-consultant" were subject to Section 1090. The sub-consultants who provided technical input, reports, and similar information in a support role to the consultants, were not subject to Section 1090.

     Generally, an independent contractor is prohibited from a subsequent project to complete a project only if the contractor has had extensive involvement in the initial design and develop of the project, which provides the contractor with considerable or undue influence over the agencies contracting decisions.

B.     SOM is Not Subject to Section 1090.

     Based on the facts provided, SOM did not influence or participate in the making of a new contract through its performance of the first contract. To the contrary, the University decided on its own to cut project costs and enter a contract with Mode Associates to change the project plans. The changes were so significant that the current proposed project is almost entirely different than the project SOM studied. Among other things: (1) the estimated project cost changed from $300M to $150M; (2) the gross square footage changed from 162,000 to 105,000; and (3) the building site changed from the northeast portion of the campus to the site of the existing science building.

     Significantly, the current project is so dissimilar from the original project that SOM's Feasibility Study will not be included in the RFP. The SOM Feasibility Study is no longer the

File No. A-19-099
Page No. 4

defining document for the current project. SOM's study will not be provided, used or needed by any
contractor submitting a bid or performing work on the project.

Additionally, there are no facts to support that SOM was involved in the University's
decisions to change the project or work with Mode Associates to make project revisions. The facts
provided do not support that SOM had the potential to exert considerable or undue influence in the
University's contracting decisions, or was engaged in the development of the RFP. Accordingly,
Section 1090 does not prohibit the University from entering the subsequent contract with SOM.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: [signature] John M. Feser Jr.
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JMFJR:aja

programming and a conceptual design study (Feasibility Study) for an academic building, but the 
project has changed significantly since SOM's Feasibility Study. 
·
